elements.sent-2411-001: The impact of machine translation (MT) on low-resource languages remains poorly understood.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, observational studies of actual usage patterns are scarce.
elements.sent-2411-003: Such studies could provide valuable insights into user needs and behaviours, complementing survey-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here we present an observational analysis of real-world MT usage for Tetun, the lingua franca of Timor-Leste, using server logs from a widely-used MT service with over $70,000$ monthly active users.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our analysis of $100,000$ translation requests reveals patterns that challenge assumptions based on existing corpora.
elements.sent-2411-006: We find that users, many of them students on mobile devices, typically translate short texts into Tetun across diverse domains including science, healthcare, and daily life.
elements.sent-2411-007: This contrasts sharply with available Tetun corpora, which are dominated by news articles covering government and social issues.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results suggest that MT systems for languages like Tetun should prioritise translating into the low-resource language, handling brief inputs effectively, and covering a wide range of domains relevant to educational contexts.
elements.sent-2411-009: More broadly, this study demonstrates how observational analysis can inform low-resource language technology development, by grounding research in practical community needs.
elements.sent-2411-010: While machine translation (MT) is often considered a single area for natural language processing (NLP), it covers a large range of applications, end-user requirements, and language directions.
elements.sent-2411-011: MT can be leveraged for everyday communication, learning, or translation of official communication \cite{dew_development_2018, vieira_understanding_2021, herrera-espejel_use_2023, paterson2023machine .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It can be used by students in the classroom \cite{deng_systematic_2022 , workers in an office environment \cite{brynjolfsson2019does , or by foreign travellers in public spaces \cite{carvalho2023attitudes .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Its reliability varies significantly depending on the size and domain coverage of available corpora \cite{dabre2020survey, khiu-etal-2024-predicting , and similarity between source and target languages \cite{xu2020boosting .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{MT_what_for_figure.jpg} \caption{Real MT usage collides with assumptions made from available corpora} \label{fig:real-vs-corpora}
elements.sent-2411-014: This disparity of potential applications and accuracy across domains or language pairs is particularly salient when translating to or from low-resource languages \cite{haddow_survey_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In low-resource scenarios, the combination of high variability in MT accuracy across domains \cite{haddow_survey_2022 , and high variability of what native speakers value in NLP applications \cite{lent_what_2022 , has prompted NLP researchers to better anchor their work in local community perception, including through surveys, focus groups, and product design \cite{mager_ethical_2023, blaschke_what_2024, le_ferrand_fashioning_2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These approaches, however, could be complemented by observational studies, that rely on end-user behaviours to infer needs or lack thereof for MT across domains or~demographics.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper on low-resource MT, we work with the Tetun language, which is the most spoken language in Timor-Leste \cite{Census2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: We rely on a sample of server logs from a volunteer-run MT service for the Tetun language, available in both website and app format.
elements.sent-2411-019: The service has over $70,000$ monthly active users, and translates over one million documents per month.
elements.sent-2411-020: From a sample of $100,000$ end-user translation text inputs, and their associated machine-translated output, we aim to respond to the following research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-021: Who are the main users of this service, what technology do they rely on, and what is the driver of their MT usage?.
elements.sent-2411-022: What characterises MT inputs, in terms of domain, length, direction of translation, and language pairs?.
elements.sent-2411-023: How do MT inputs compare to available corpora for the Tetun language?.
elements.sent-2411-025: While Tetun is a low-resource language \cite{de_jesus_text_2024 , as of the 2015 census, it was the largest mother tongue in Timor-Leste with 361,027 speakers, and an additional 571,406 speakers of Tetun as a second language \cite{Census2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: It is the lingua franca in this country, spoken in most public spaces, and in the media \cite{greksakova_tetun_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: To our knowledge, the majority of Tetun NLP research to date was published by Gabriel de Jesus during his PhD candidature at the University of Porto.
elements.sent-2411-028: This included the creation of a language identification model, language tokenizer, and data pipeline for gathering Tetun monolingual data \cite{de-jesus-nunes-2024-labadain-crawler , the publication of that corpus \cite{de_jesus_labadain-30k_2024 , the study of retrieval strategies for Tetun text \cite{de_jesus_text_2024 , and the study of different large language models (LLMs) relevance judgement capabilities in Tetun \cite{de_jesus_exploring_2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Community-driven approaches to NLP for low-resource languages.
elements.sent-2411-030: Recent years have witnessed a growing focus on developing NLP technologies for low-resource and endangered languages.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, researchers have identified that academic motivations can be at odds with the actual needs of language communities \cite{le_ferrand_fashioning_2022, liu_not_2022 , which made them advocate for a paradigm shift towards more community-centered approaches in NLP research \cite{lent_what_2022, bird_centering_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Studies emphasise the importance of close collaboration with language communities, understanding their specific needs, and involving them in the design and development process of language technologies \cite{mager_ethical_2023, bird_centering_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: This approach acknowledges the heterogeneity among low-resource languages and their communities, recognising that methodologies effective for one may not be universally applicable \cite{lent_what_2022, dolinska_akha_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Linguistic analysis through large-scale data gathering.
elements.sent-2411-035: To our knowledge, this paper is the first large-scale analysis of real-world usage patterns in MT.
elements.sent-2411-036: It takes a similar approach to prior work leveraging large-scale secondary data in linguistics, including those predicting language learning difficulty \cite{schepens_big_2020 , understanding what language characteristics lead to a language being confused for another \cite{skirgard_why_2017 , and games that aim to incentivise the development of new language resources \cite{fiumara_nieuw_2022 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-118: Our results seem to indicate that future work on Tetun MT should focus on: (1) broad domain coverage that would include texts about daily life, science and health, (2) accuracy when translating into Tetun rather than from Tetun, and (3) accuracy when translating very short documents (including individual words).

elements.sent-2411-130: Lastly, we identify some potential shortcomings of our domain classification methodology: (1) some classes are overlapping (e.g. text about biology could fall under "Health \& medicine" or "Science \& technology"), which makes them harder to distinguish, (2) it does not identify the source of each text, e.g. a document about healthcare could be from a news article or from a research article, and (3) it works only on documents that longer than 5 words, but most translated documents are very short, thus not covered by our classification methodology.
elements.sent-2411-131: We present an analysis of MT usage for Tetun, a low-resource language, based on server logs from a widely-used MT service.
elements.sent-2411-132: Our study reveals key insights into user demographics, device preferences, translation directions, and domain coverage.
elements.sent-2411-133: We find that a large share of users are likely students in Timor-Leste using mobile devices, with a preference for translating into Tetun rather than from it.
elements.sent-2411-134: Our analysis also uncovers a significant mismatch between the domains of user-translated text and existing Tetun corpora.
elements.sent-2411-135: Our recommendations include a stronger emphasis on domains that are not well covered by existing corpora (in particular domains that are important to education), accuracy for translation into Tetun, performance on very short texts, and the development of mobile-optimised MT solutions.
elements.sent-2411-136: For the HCI community, we suggest focusing on MT perception and improvement for students in low-resource contexts.
elements.sent-2411-137: This study provides a first observational analysis of real-world MT usage for a low-resource language.
elements.sent-2411-138: It contributes to a more nuanced understanding of the challenges and opportunities in this field.
elements.sent-2411-139: We hope these insights will guide future research and development efforts, ultimately leading to more effective and accessible MT solutions for speakers of low-resource languages.. .

elements.sent-2411-140: Low-resource machine translation (MT) presents a diversity of community needs and application challenges that remain poorly understood.
elements.sent-2411-141: To complement surveys and focus groups, which tend to rely on small samples of respondents, we propose an observational study on actual usage patterns of a specialized MT service for the Tetun language, which is the lingua franca in Timor-Leste.
elements.sent-2411-142: We find that users, many of them students on mobile devices, typically translate text from a high-resource language into Tetun across diverse domains including science, healthcare, and daily life.
elements.sent-2411-143: Our results suggest that MT systems for minority languages like Tetun should prioritize accuracy on domains relevant to educational contexts, in the high-resource to low-resource direction.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Effects of domain mismatch on LM modelling
elements.sent-2411-206: After uncovering a lack of similarity between MT inputs and available corpora in the previous section, we investigate downstream effects on language modelling, by comparing perplexity on MT Tetun inputs with perplexity on monolingual corpora using Goldfish Tetun\footnote{\href{https://huggingface.co/goldfish-models/tet_latn_full {https://huggingface.co/goldfish-models/tet\_latn\_full \cite{chang_goldfish_2024 , a monolingual Tetun language model trained mostly (> 98\%) on MADLAD Tetun documents.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-207: We use the HuggingFace evaluate\footnote{\href{https://github.com/huggingface/evaluate {https://github.com/huggingface/evaluate , version 0.4.3 library for all perplexity measurements, and only measure on inputs of 5 words or more.}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: We compare perplexity on MT Tetun inputs with perplexity on the Labadain Tetun corpus \cite{de_jesus_labadain-30k_2024 , which is the second largest Tetun corpus after MADLAD, and was not used when training the Goldfish model.}
elements.sent-2411-209: We also exclude from Labadain sentences that are also present in MADLAD.
elements.sent-2411-210: We get an average perplexity of $1,774$ on MT Tetun inputs, compared to an average of $153$ for Labadain, which illustrates that MT inputs may contain vocabulary, structures, or topics that are underrepresented in scraped monolingual corpora, leading to lower language modelling performance and potentially increased translation errors.
elements.sent-2411-211: Comparing average perplexity per topic, we find that documents in the ``Government \& socio-economic issues'' topic have a much lower perplexity (average of $632$) than documents from the ``Technology'', ``Business \& work \& employment'', or ``Daily life \& personal experiences'' domains ($3,434$, $1,871$ and $1,864$ respectively).
elements.sent-2411-212: This result illustrates that domain imbalance in monolingual corpora has downstream effect on language modelling ability for under-represented domains.
elements.sent-2411-213: Comparing average perplexity per provenance, we find that documents that come from ``News article \& press release'' have the lowest perplexity at $874$, compared to $1,320$ for ``Education \& research material'', $1,662$ for ``Organizational \& formal documents'' and $1,865$ for ``Conversation \& correspondence''.
elements.sent-2411-214: Here also, this result shows that document provenances that are under-represented in available corpora impact language modelling performance.
elements.sent-2411-215: We report all perplexity results in Appendix~\ref{sec:perplexity-results .}
elements.paragraph-2411-021: Anchoring MT evaluation in user demand.
